content.text: Saying what to do instead of what not to do
is a core principle of effective prompt engineering. Instead of just saying what not to do, tell the model explicitly what to do instead. This is rule #7 in OpenAI's official best practices guide.

attrs.alt: Say What To Do, Not What Not To Do diagram

content.text: The key insight
: Models respond better to
positive phrasing
("Do this") rather than
negative phrasing
("Don't do that").
Why This Works
When you tell a model
what to do
, you give it a clear action to follow. When you tell it
what not to do
, you create ambiguity about what alternatives are available.
Research-backed principle
: "Use positive phrasing ('Do this' instead of 'Don't do that')" is one of the core rules for getting better AI results.
Models are trained to
generate text
, not to avoid it. Saying "don't write X" is harder to follow than "write Y instead."
The Core Principle
Negative Phrasing (Bad)
DO NOT ASK USERNAME OR PASSWORD.
Positive Phrasing (Good)

The agent will attempt to diagnose the problem and suggest a solution, whilst refraining from asking any questions related to PII.
Instead of asking for PII, such as username or password, refer the user to the help article www.samplewebsite.com/help/faq
The positive version tells the model
exactly what to do
instead: refer the user to the help article.
Real Examples: Before & After
Example 1: Customer Support Bot
❌ Bad (Negative):
DO NOT ASK FOR USERNAME OR PASSWORD.
✅ Good (Positive):
Instead of asking for PII, such as username or password, refer the user to the help article www.samplewebsite.com/help/faq
The agent will attempt to diagnose the problem and suggest a solution, whilst refraining from asking any questions related to PII.
Example 2: Product Description Length
❌ Bad (Vague + Negative):
"The description for this product should be fairly short, a few sentences only, and not too much more."
✅ Good (Specific + Positive):
"Use a 3 to 5 sentence paragraph to describe this product."
Be specific about the context, outcome, length, format, style, etc.
Example 3: Writing Style
❌ Bad (Negative):

"Don't use technical jargon."
"Don't write in passive voice."
"Don't make it too long."
✅ Good (Positive):

"Write for high schoolers using simple language."
"Use active voice."
"Keep it under 150 words."
Give clear instructions about what to do.
Example 4: Code Quality
❌ Bad (Negative):

"Don't use external libraries."
"Don't invent APIs not in the codebase."
"Don't remove existing tests."
✅ Good (Positive):

"Use only libraries already in the codebase."
"Follow existing patterns in the repo."
"Never remove or weaken existing tests."
Specify what to do with explicit constraints.
Example 5: Email Tone
❌ Bad (Negative):

"Don't be too formal."
"Don't sound robotic."
"Don't use exclamation marks."
✅ Good (Positive):

"Write a professional but friendly email."
"Use conversational language."
"Use a supportive, encouraging tone."
Define your target audience and use positive phrasing.
Example 6: Content Summary
❌ Bad (Negative):
"Summarize this text. Don't include every detail. Don't make it too long."
✅ Good (Positive):
"Summarize the text below as a bullet point list of the most important points."
Be specific, descriptive, and as detailed as possible about the desired outcome, length, format, style.
Example 7: Entity Extraction
❌ Bad (Negative):
"Extract the important entities mentioned in the text below. Don't forget company names. Don't skip people names."
✅ Good (Positive):

"Extract the important entities mentioned in the text below.
First extract all company names, then extract all people names, then extract specific topics which fit the content and finally extract general overarching themes.

Desired format:
Company names: <comma_separated_list_of_company_names>
People names: -||-
Specific topics: -||-
General themes: -||-"
Articulate the desired output format through examples. Show, and tell - the models respond better when shown specific format requirements.
Why Positive Phrasing Works Better
Clear Action vs. Ambiguity
Negative
: "Don't write long paragraphs" → What's the alternative?
Positive
: "Use short paragraphs (3-4 sentences each)" → Clear action to follow
Models Generate Text, Not Avoid It
LLMs are trained to
generate text
, not to suppress it. Saying "don't write X" requires the model to inhibit its natural behavior. Saying "write Y instead" guides it toward a specific output.
Positive Framing Reduces Cognitive Load

When you say "don't do X," the model has to:
1. Understand what X is
2. Inhibit generating X
3. Guess what to do instead

When you say "do Y," the model just needs to:
1. Understand what Y is
2. Generate Y
Avoids Negative Bias

Negative phrasing can create unintended bias. For example:
- "Don't be biased" → Might cause the model to overcorrect
- "Be balanced and consider multiple perspectives" → Clear guidance
Best Practices for Positive Phrasing
Use "Do This" Instead of "Don't Do That"
❌ Bad:
"Don't use passive voice."
✅ Good:
"Use active voice."
Use positive phrasing ("Do this" instead of "Don't do that").
Replace "Not" with Specific Alternatives
❌ Bad:
"Don't make it too long."
✅ Good:
"Keep it under 150 words."
Be specific, descriptive and as detailed as possible about the desired context, outcome, length, format, style, etc.
Give Explicit Instructions
❌ Bad:
"Write something about dogs."
✅ Good:
"List 5 unique facts about Golden Retrievers, in bullet points."
Give clear instructions.
Specify What To Include
❌ Bad:
"Extract entities. Don't miss company names."
✅ Good:
"Extract the following 4 entity types: company names, people names, specific topics and themes."
Be specific about what to extract.
Use Positive Constraints
❌ Bad:

"Don't use external libraries."
"Don't invent new APIs."
✅ Good:

"Use only libraries already in the codebase."
"Follow existing patterns in the repo."
Specify explicit constraints about what to do.
Common Mistakes
Mistake 1: Overusing "Don't"
❌ Bad:

"Don't be too formal."
"Don't write long paragraphs."
"Don't use jargon."
"Don't make it too complex."
✅ Good:

"Be friendly and conversational."
"Use short paragraphs (3-4 sentences each)."
"Use simple language suitable for high schoolers."
"Keep explanations clear and straightforward."
Mistake 2: Vague Negative Instructions
❌ Bad:

"Don't make it too long."
"Don't be too brief."
"Don't be too formal."
✅ Good:

"Keep it under 150 words."
"Write a 3-paragraph email."
"Use a professional but friendly tone."
Mistake 3: Implied Negatives
❌ Bad:

"Make sure it's not too complicated."
"Ensure it's not too simple."
✅ Good:

"Explain for a beginner in [field]."
"Explain it to me like I'm 11 years old."
Key insight
: Models respond better to
positive phrasing
("Do this") rather than
negative phrasing
("Don't do that").
Why it works
Clear action vs. ambiguity

— Gives the model a specific action to follow
2.
Models generate text, not avoid it

— LLMs are trained to generate text, not suppress it
3.
Reduces cognitive load

— Model doesn't need to guess alternatives
4.
Avoids negative bias
— Prevents overcorrection
Best practices
Use "Do this" instead of "Don't do that"

— Positive phrasing
2.
Replace "not" with specific alternatives

— "Keep under 150 words" vs. "don't make it too long"
3.
Give explicit instructions

— "List 5 facts" vs. "write something"
4.
Specify what to include

— "Extract company names, people names, topics" vs. "don't miss names"
5.
Use positive constraints
— "Use only existing libraries" vs. "don't use external libraries"
Instead of just saying what not to do,
say what to do instead
. This is rule #7 in OpenAI's official best practices guide and one of the most impactful techniques for improving prompt quality.
How It Works in Practice
Understanding the internals of say what to do, not what not to do helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Best Practices
Production experience with say what to do, not what not to do has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Common Pitfalls
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Advanced Best Practices

Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.

Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Advanced Common Pitfalls
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.

Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Best Practices
Following best practices ensures effective use of Say What To Do, Not What Not To Do in real-world applications.
Start with the simplest approach that solves your problem before adding complexity.
Test edge cases thoroughly, especially boundary conditions and error states.
Document your implementation decisions for future maintainability.
Monitor performance metrics and optimize based on actual usage patterns.
Follow security best practices and validate all inputs at system boundaries.
Use version control and code reviews for all production changes.
Establish clear error handling strategies before deployment.
Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
Understanding typical pitfalls in Say What To Do, Not What Not To Do helps you avoid costly errors.
Over-engineering solutions when simpler approaches would suffice — start minimal.
Neglecting error handling, leading to silent failures in production.
Skipping testing because the implementation appears straightforward.
Ignoring rate limits and quotas when designing at scale.
Not considering the user experience impact of latency or error states.
Key Takeaways
Here are the most important concepts from this section on Say What To Do, Not What Not To Do:
Understanding the core concepts is essential before building on top of them.
Practice by implementing small examples before tackling complex scenarios.
The official documentation is your best reference for up-to-date information.
Community resources and examples provide practical guidance beyond documentation.
Practical Implementation Guide
A step-by-step approach to implementing Say What To Do, Not What Not To Do in your projects.
Assess your requirements: Define clear goals, constraints, and success criteria.
Design the architecture: Plan how components interact before writing code.
Implement incrementally: Build a minimal working version first, then iterate.
Test comprehensively: Cover both happy paths and error scenarios.
Deploy carefully: Use staging environments and gradual rollouts.
Monitor and iterate: Collect metrics and improve based on real-world usage.
Testing Strategies
Effective testing ensures your Say What To Do, Not What Not To Do implementation works correctly under all conditions.
Unit tests: Verify individual functions and components in isolation.
Integration tests: Confirm components work correctly together.
End-to-end tests: Validate complete user workflows from start to finish.
Performance tests: Ensure acceptable response times under load.
Chaos testing: Verify resilience by intentionally introducing failures.
Summary

This section covered Say What To Do, Not What Not To Do in depth, including core concepts, practical implementation approaches, and production considerations. Apply these patterns consistently to build robust, maintainable systems.
